coredata.dc:title: Bionic PDMS-CDs surface with thermal controllable adhesion
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Xudong
Chen, Pei
Qin, Fei
Dong, Xiaoxiao
Xu, Quan
coredata.dc:description: In the process of temporary bonding and debonding of a thin wafer, it is necessary to ensure that the bonding layer has reliable adhesion property, and the adhesion process should be controllable for debonding. We propose a wafer bonding layer based on gecko-inspired bionic adhesion structure, which is composed of carbon quantum dots (CDs) and polydimethylsiloxane (PDMS), and has a single-layer micro-pillar array on the surface. Compared with the adhesion layer made from pure PDMS, the PDMS-CDs surface can greatly improve the adhesion strength. Moreover, doped CDs makes the bionic surface sensitive to temperature, therefore, its surface adhesion is controllable by varying temperature. With the increase in temperature, the surface adhesion decreases significantly, which makes debonding possibly. In this study, the surface adhesion properties of PDMS-CDs were studied at both millimeter and nanometer scale under variable temperature conditions.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Bionic surface
Thermal controllable adhesion
Nanoparticles
Contacts
Temporary bonding

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) SEM image of the bionic surface of PDMS-CDs; (b) enlarged SEM image of the bionic surface of PDMS-CDs; (c) schematic of the homemade adhesion testing fixture; (d) digital image of the fixture mounted on UTM.

Adhesion force vs displacement in UTM for 0% PDMS-CDs sample (a), 6% PDMS-CDs sample (b) and 10% PDMS-CDs sample (c); (d) temperature vs adhesion strength for sample a, b and c in millimeter scale; (e) temperature vs adhesion force for sample a, b and c by AFM in nanometer scale.

(a) Contact angle of water on the bionic surface in 30 °C; (b) contact angle of water on the bionic surface in 50 °C; (c) Surface adhesion work of sample a, b and c at different temperatures; (d) FTIR of sample a, b and c at 30 °C (e) FTIR of sample c at 30 °C and 50 °C; (f) schematics of high CDs doping effect; (g) schematics of low CDs doping concentration, and (h) schematics of high temperatures effect.
Contact angle test results of bionic surface with ethylene glycol.

head.ce:title: Bionic PDMS-CDs surface with thermal controllable adhesion

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Electronics Packaging Technology & Reliability, College of Mechanical Engineering & Applied Electronics Technology, Beijing University of Technology, Beijing 100124, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Institute of Electronics Packaging Technology & Reliability
College of Mechanical Engineering & Applied Electronics Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Institute of Electronics Packaging Technology & Reliability, College of Mechanical Engineering & Applied Electronics Technology, Beijing University of Technology, Beijing 100124, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Heavy Oil Processing, China University of Petroleum, Beijing 102249, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Heavy Oil Processing

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory of Heavy Oil Processing, China University of Petroleum, Beijing 102249, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In the process of temporary bonding and debonding of a thin wafer, it is necessary to ensure that the bonding layer has reliable adhesion property, and the adhesion process should be controllable for debonding. We propose a wafer bonding layer based on gecko-inspired bionic adhesion structure, which is composed of carbon quantum dots (CDs) and polydimethylsiloxane (PDMS), and has a single-layer micro-pillar array on the surface. Compared with the adhesion layer made from pure PDMS, the PDMS-CDs surface can greatly improve the adhesion strength. Moreover, doped CDs makes the bionic surface sensitive to temperature, therefore, its surface adhesion is controllable by varying temperature. With the increase in temperature, the surface adhesion decreases significantly, which makes debonding possibly. In this study, the surface adhesion properties of PDMS-CDs were studied at both millimeter and nanometer scale under variable temperature conditions.

ce:section.ce:para: During the wafers processing, the device wafer needs to be temporarily bonded on the carrier wafers to improve their strength and protect the surface structure and circuits, which needs to be debonded when the process is completed [1]. At present, debonding of wafers is mainly realized by thermal slip, mechanical debonding and laser debonding, etc. Among the most widely used thermal slip debonding, it is usually necessary to heat the bonding layer to 220 °C − 240 °C to reach the melting point of the bonding material, and then slip to untie the bonding connection. The process steps are complex, the cost is high, and large thermal or mechanical stress will be introduced to the thin wafers, resulting in warpage, fracture and other damage of wafer [2]. A low-temperature, easy debonding process is desired.

Gecko-inspired surface usually has strong adhesion, easy detachment, and self-cleaning effect, which has a strong potential in the application of wafer temporary bonding [3]. Geckos can control its adhesion and detachment with ease through a critical angle between the adhesive fibrils and the contact surfaces [4]. When the angle between the setal stalk and the contact surface is less than 30°, the gecko's pad will show strong adhesion to facilitate attachment [5]. The controllable adhesion characteristics are mainly controlled by the anisotropy of the surface structure. Its self-cleaning effect ensures the successful shedding of surface pollutants [6–9]. Wang et al. [10] fabricated the bionic surfaces in which droplets move in different directions at different temperatures to achieve temperature controllable adhesion. In order to improve the bionic surface adhesion performance, many studies are devoted to the doping of a variety of nanoscale materials to improve their surface adhesion or provide the means of adhesion control [11]. The CDs, as a new type of zero-dimensional nanomaterials, has excellent photothermal effect and photocatalytic properties [12] and has great potential in the adhesion control and adhesion improvement of bionic adhesion surface as doped materials [13,14].

We present a type of new temporary bonding layered material with pillar structure, which is assembled from PDMS matrix and CDs filler. In our study, the samples of different concentrations of CDs in PDMS are prepared. Ultimate tensile machine (UTM) and atomic force microscopy (AFM) are adopted to study the adhesion properties at different length-scale. With a temperature control module, the adhesion forces under different temperatures are also studied to illustrate the temperature controllable adhesion property.

The CDs solution is produced from a mixture of sodium citrate, calcium chloride powder urea and toluene solvent. After fully mixing, the mixture was transferred to autoclave for 36 h heat preservation under 160 °C. After that, 0.22 mm cylindrical membrane filter was used to filter the mixture to obtain the final CDs solution. The PDMS (Sylgard184) is mixed with the curing agent at a ratio of 10:1. We used three samples with different CDs and PDMS mass fraction, namely 0% (sample a), 6% (sample b), and 10% (sample c). After mixing the CDs solution with PDMS solution, the PDMS-CDs solutions need to be poured to a pre-designed silicon template for 10 h curing under 55 °C. After fully cured, then demoulded from the template, the bionic surface is formed, as shown in Fig. 1 (a) and (b). The structure is a 10 mm by 10 mm square with a cylindrical structure with a diameter of about 15 μm and a height of about 17 μm, and the depth to width ratio is about 1.13.

In order to measure the adhesion force between PDMS-CDs bionic adhesion surface and silicon wafer at millimeter scale, a pull-off fixture based on the UTM was designed, as shown in Fig. 1 (c) and (d). A slide of silicon wafer of 15 mm by 15 mm was fixed on upper fixture. The lower fixture has a temperature control module inside, and the PDMS-CDs sample is faced up to contact with the silicon wafer. In the test, the pre-pressure is 1 N, and the separation speed is 10 μm/s.

AFM is also adopted to test the adhesion force at nanometer scale, and SiO2 half-sphere tip was used. The detailed test scheme was introduced in our previous study [15]. Contact angle test and FTIR under different temperatures are also conducted to understand the adhesion mechanism.

As shown in Fig. 2 (a–c), when the pull-off displacement increases, the adhesion force between the bionic surface and the silicon wafer gradually increases in the initial stage, which means the elastic or plastic deformation of the sample. When the adhesion force reaches a plateau, the increased displacement means the separation between two surfaces. With the further increase of the pull-off displacement, the bionic surface and the silicon wafer get out of the adhesive state. The temperature dependence is observed for all three samples.
To compare the temperature effect and CD doping effect clearly, the macroscopic adhesion strength was calculated from Fig. 2 (a)–(c) by divided the maximum adhesion force by contact area.

As shown in Fig. 2 (d), with the temperature rising from 30 °C to 50 °C, the adhesion strength of the bionic surface decreased gradually, and the high doping concentration (samples c) exhibits more significant temperature effect. In detail, the macroscopic adhesion strength at 50 °C of sample a, b and c decreased by 50.54%, 59.89%, and 70.66% respectively compared with that at 30 °C. The influence of CDs of doping concentrations on the adhesion is also evident. As shown in Fig. 2 (d), compared with that of pure PDMS sample a, he adhesion strength of sample c increased in the range of 559.96% to 1012.66% at the temperature of 30 °C to 50 °C. AFM was used to study the microscopic adhesion properties of bionic surface. As shown in Fig. 2 (e), the microscopic adhesion force also has the similar CDs doping effect and temperature effect.

The degradation of adhesion performance is related the change of its surface adhesion work. In previous literature, surface adhesion work has been described as Eq. (1) [16]:
(1)
W a
=
γ lg
1 + cos θ

Wa is the surface adhesion work, γ lg is the surface tension of the test liquid, and cosθ is the contact angle between the test liquid and the bionic surface. Ethylene glycol was used for the contact angle measurement, and the test results are shown in Table 1 . As shown in Fig. 3 , at 30 °C, 40 °C, and 50 °C, the adhesion work of sample c increased by 22.46%, 23.55% and 71.58% from sample a respectively. It shows that the increase of the doping concentration of CDs can improve the adhesion work under the same test conditions. All three samples exhibit similar temperature effect, and high doping sample didn’t show significant differentia. The reason could be explained from the distinctive mechanism of the two tests.

The contact angle test determined the adhesion energy from the surface wettability of the tested surface with liquid, which is also known as surface energy. Since the contact surface is mostly from PDMS, which contributed to the majority of surface energy. Compare with the contact area of PDMS, the variation of CDs plays a minimal role in surface contact, therefore, the influence on surface energy is minor, too. However, the adhesion strength obtained from UTS debonding test is the overall evaluation of the adhesion performance of bionic surface. Beside of the surface energy, the adhesion strength could be improved by other forms of contact force, such as intermolecular force (van der Waals forces), interatomic force (hydrogen bonds) etc. These forces can’t be detected by contact angle test, because the contact angle test is a liquid-solid contact. These forces may directly affect the adhesion performance which could be dominated by the concertation of CDs. Therefore, the effect of CDs will be further investigated by FTIR.

As a silicon-based polymer material, the silicon-hydrogen bond on the surface of PDMS is easy to combine with –OH to form hydrogen bond, thus making the physical binding forces on the bionic surface (hydrogen bond) increase, resulting in the increase of surface adhesion strength. In Fig. 3 (d), the peak strength at 3450 cm−1 gradually increases, and there is no new peak formation around, proving that the peak concentration of the –OH group represented by this wavenumber increases. In Fig. 3 (e), the increase of temperature leads to the decrease of wave peak at wavenumber 3450 cm−1, therefore, the adhesion strength decreases accordingly. At this point, the doping of CDs plays a leading role in the application of bionic surface.

In our study, a temperature-based bionic controllable adhesion surface is proposed and studied in millimeter scale and nanometer scale by UTM and AFM. It was found that when the temperature rose from 30 °C to 50 °C, the adhesion strength of the surface decreased significantly, and the maximum decrease was 70.66% of its initial value in millimeter scale, and the adhesion force obtained by AFM test also showed the same trend. Contact angle tests proved the increase of CDs concentration can improve the surface adhesion work, and the increase of temperature can degrade the surface adhesion work. FTIR experiment detected concentration of the –OH group is inflected by CDs concentration and temperature increase. Overall, the doping of CDs to PDMS can significantly improve the adhesion strength of the prepared bionic surface, which is also controllable by temperature change.

Xudong Li: Investigation, Validation, Writing - original draft. Pei Chen: Supervision, Methodology, Writing - review & editing. Fei Qin: Supervision, Funding acquisition. Xiaoxiao Dong: Resources, Data curation. Quan Xu: Conceptualization, Project administration.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We thank National Natural Science Foundation of China No. 51878877, 11502005 and Science Foundation of China University of Petroleum (No. 2462019QNXZ02) for financial support.
